name: Dumbbell layout
description: Floor-layout strategy in which two bulky principal rooms of roughly equal footprint anchor the two short ends of a long, narrow plan, joined in the middle by a much narrower corridor or gallery that pinches the building's waist. The two end rooms — typically a great hall at one pole and a master suite, solar or formal dining chamber at the other — read as the public and private gravity wells of the dwelling, while the slender connector enforces a deliberate procession between them. This pattern underlies Tudor manor houses where great hall and parlour bookend a screens passage, Renaissance enfilades that line state apartments along a single sight axis, and English country-house double-pile plans whose end pavilions are tied by a glazed gallery.
placement_rules: Align the long axis of the plan with the two end-room centroids so the connector reads as a single straight pinch between them.
Size the two end rooms within 10% of each other in both width and depth so neither pole visually dominates the dumbbell silhouette.
Keep the connector width at 2 blocks for domestic galleries; widen to 3 only for ceremonial enfilades that must allow two-abreast procession.
Connector length should be at least 4 blocks and no more than the depth of either end room, otherwise the waist either disappears or reads as a separate third room.
The connector's external walls step inward from the end-room walls by 2 to 4 blocks on each side, producing the visible pinch in plan and on the facade.
Place the single doorway between each end room and the connector on the long axis so the enfilade sight-line runs uninterrupted end-to-end.
Light the connector with windows on both long walls, since the inward step exposes both sides to the exterior.
lighting_ventilation: End rooms take their main light from large windows on the short outer walls and from the two flank walls that extend past the pinch. The narrow connector is lit from both long sides at once thanks to the inward step, often with a continuous band of windows or a glazed gallery; supplement with lanterns at the midpoint for evening use.

medieval.notes: Tudor manor reading: great hall at one end with open timber roof, solar or parlour at the other, joined by a low screens passage with stone walls and timber-framed ceiling.

renaissance.notes: Enfilade reading: state apartments at each pole, linked by a long gallery with symmetrical pilastered walls and an unbroken axial sight-line door-to-door.
